skill.name: triaging-issues
skill.description: GitHub issue triage support for classifying issues, finding duplicates, mapping relationships, validating issue quality, and applying repository workflow actions with helper scripts.
skill.summary: Triage GitHub issues, detect duplicates, map dependencies, and improve issue quality.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows most are false positives from markdown examples, common issue-triage words, placeholders, and standard shell redirection. The real residual risk is operational: helper scripts execute authenticated GitHub CLI commands that can edit labels, close issues, comment, and add issues to projects. No evidence found of credential theft, prompt injection, hidden network beacons, malicious code execution, or data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated GitHub Issue Mutations
medium_findings.description: The helper script can label issues, close duplicate issues, add comments, and add issues to projects through authenticated GitHub CLI commands. This matches the skill purpose, but broad filters or incorrect issue numbers can change repository state at scale.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly call gh issue edit, gh issue close, gh issue comment, and gh project item-add. The behavior is legitimate for triage automation but can alter live repository state.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution Through GitHub CLI
medium_findings.description: The shell and Python helpers execute gh, jq, grep, date, and related local commands to read and process issue data. Arguments are passed as command arguments rather than eval strings, so command injection evidence is low, but the skill still depends on external command execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Static command-execution patterns are present and confirmed semantically. The commands are expected for GitHub issue workflows and do not show shell eval or secret exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static False Positives From Markdown and Triage Vocabulary
low_findings.description: Many high static findings are false positives caused by markdown code fences, issue lifecycle terms such as Blocked, and issue template placeholders. No weak cryptographic algorithm use or C2 behavior was found in these reviewed locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files contain documentation diagrams, issue templates, and ordinary triage words. They do not contain cryptographic code, shell execution outside examples, or malicious control flow.

low_findings.title: Benign Device Redirection In Shell Helpers
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings point to standard /dev/null redirection used to suppress command output. This is a common shell pattern and does not read sensitive files or traverse paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced shell lines use output redirection or suppressed errors around GitHub CLI operations. No evidence found of sensitive filesystem reads or arbitrary path construction.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bulk Operations Over User-Supplied GitHub Search Filters
dangerous_patterns.description: Batch helpers accept search filters and then apply labels or project changes to every returned issue. This can be useful, but users should preview matches before running write actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly performs repeated write operations based on search results. The risk is operational misuse, not malicious hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Issue Closure Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The close-duplicate helper closes one issue and comments on another issue. This action should require human confirmation when used in production repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both the script and instructions show duplicate closure workflows. The pattern is expected but can affect public project records.

content.value_statement: Growing issue queues hide duplicates, blockers, and unclear reports. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code classify issues, find relationships, and produce actionable triage recommendations.
actual_capabilities: Classifies GitHub issues by type, priority, scope, and effort using documented triage rules.
Searches for likely duplicate issues with GitHub CLI queries and simple similarity checks.
Maps issue relationships such as blocked-by, related, duplicate, and dependency references.
Validates issue quality by checking titles, descriptions, labels, reproduction steps, and environment details.
Provides reusable response templates for duplicates, missing information, security reports, and stale issues.
Includes helper scripts for batch labeling, duplicate closure, project assignment, and triage reporting.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI authentication and repository permissions for live issue operations.
Duplicate detection uses simple keyword and title matching, not a deep semantic model.
Bulk write actions can affect many issues if filters are too broad.
The skill does not replace maintainer judgment for priority, ownership, or closure decisions.

use_cases.title: Daily Backlog Triage
use_cases.description: Review new issues, apply labels, identify missing information, and prepare a short action summary for maintainers.

use_cases.title: Duplicate Cleanup
use_cases.description: Compare similar reports, rank possible duplicates, and recommend which issue should remain canonical.

use_cases.title: Dependency Planning
use_cases.description: Map blocked issues and related work so planning teams can choose the next actionable items.

prompt_templates.title: Triage One Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Triage GitHub issue #[number]. Classify its type, priority, scope, quality, duplicate risk, and recommended next action.

prompt_templates.title: Find Possible Duplicates
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether issue #[number] is a duplicate. Compare likely matches, explain confidence, and recommend whether to close, link, or keep it open.

prompt_templates.title: Review Unlabeled Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Review all open unlabeled issues. Group them by recommended label, note missing information, and list any actions that need confirmation before writing changes.

prompt_templates.title: Map Release Blockers
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze open issues for blockers and dependencies before the next release. Identify ready work, blocked work, duplicate groups, and the recommended resolution order.

output_examples.output: The issue is likely a high-priority bug in authentication. It needs environment details and logs. A related issue exists, but it is not clearly a duplicate. Recommended actions are to add bug and priority labels, request logs, and link the related issue.

output_examples.input: Find duplicate feature requests for dark mode.
output_examples.output: Three similar requests describe the same user need. Keep the oldest issue as canonical, link the two newer reports, and preserve unique acceptance criteria before closing duplicates.

output_examples.input: What issues are blocking the next sprint?
output_examples.output: Authentication work is the main blocker because profile and password reset tasks depend on it. Documentation and session cleanup are ready to start in parallel.

best_practices: Preview issue search results before running any bulk label, close, or project update command.
Keep duplicate decisions reversible by linking issues and preserving unique reporter details.
Ask for missing reproduction steps or environment details before assigning complex bugs.
anti_patterns: Closing issues only because titles look similar without checking body content and context.
Applying broad bulk filters in repositories where labels or issue templates are inconsistent.
Treating automated priority recommendations as final without maintainer review.

faq.question: Does this skill change GitHub issues automatically?
faq.answer: It includes scripts that can change issues when run with GitHub CLI permissions. Review commands before using write actions.

faq.question: Can it detect duplicates across closed issues?
faq.answer: It can search GitHub issues with user-provided filters. Include closed issues in the search query when needed.

faq.question: Does it need repository access?
faq.answer: Read-only analysis needs issue visibility. Labeling, commenting, closing, and project updates need the matching GitHub permissions.

faq.question: How accurate is the duplicate detection?
faq.answer: It uses keyword and title comparisons as a first pass. Maintainers should confirm duplicates before closing reports.

faq.question: Can it help with security reports?
faq.answer: It provides a response template that directs sensitive reports to a private security process instead of public discussion.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
